content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

The conversation around AI-assisted development has been focused almost exclusively on productivity.
How much code can it generate? How many hours can it save? How quickly can you ship features?
Now, while there is an argument to be had that these questions are indeed valid, I feel that there is a more important point that is being missed. AI is not a replacement for software engineering fundamentals.
In fact, the developers who get the most value from AI are usually the ones who already understand architecture, design principles, debugging, testing and system design. (You may be able to spin up a decent app via Gemini, Claude or ChatGPT, but when issues arise, it will be the developers who suffer most as they navigate code manufactured by a machine that has hijacked the knowledge of developers in the first place).
The difference is not they are they using AI more. It’s that they are using AI differently.
They treat it as a safety net, a reviewer, a research assistant and an accelerator — not a substitute for thinking.
The Calculator Analogy
Consider someone who has never learned mathematics but has access to a calculator. They can perform calculations, but they will never understand:

• why the answer is correct

• whether the answer makes sense at all

• when the calculator is being used incorrectly
Now, an engineer who understands mathematics — the calculator does not replace their knowledge. It amplifies it. AI works the same way.
A developer who understands software engineering can use AI to move faster. A developer who doesn’t understand software engineering often ends up moving quickly in the wrong direction.
The Best Developers Still Design Before They Build
Rather than:
Requirement => Ask AI => Paste Code
It becomes:
Requirement => Understand Problem => Design Solution => Use AI to Accelerate
The architecture should come from you. The implementation can then be assisted by AI. If you cannot explain how a solution should work before asking AI for code, that is the real problem.
Use AI for the Boring Parts
The highest return on AI Usage often comes from removing repetitive work.
These include:

• writing unit test scaffolding

• creating DTOs

• generating documentation

• producing boilerplate code

• creating mock data

• explaining unfamiliar APIs
These are tasks that consume time but provide relatively little intelluctual value. The goal is not to avoid thinking but to spend more time thinking about important problems.
Let AI Challenge You
Experience developers understand that most bugs originate from assumptions.
Examples include:

• assuming input is valid

• assuming a service is available

• assuming data is complete

• assuming concurrency won’t matter
When working, you should consider what failure scenarios should be considered or what are the risks in a particular approach.
You should use AI to review your own thinking. Let it empower you to consider edge cases you may be missing or weaknesses in your design. This changes the relationship between a developer and AI.
AI becomes a second set of eyes rather than a code generator. This produces far better results than asking for complete implementations.
Strong Fundamentals Matter More Than Ever
The majority of the world has become convinced that AI makes traditional software engineering skills less important. I believe that the opposite is true.
As code generation becomes easier, the value shifts toward:

• architecture

• system design

• problem solving

• security

• performance

• communication

• design-making
Anyone can generate code. Not everyone can determine whether that code belongs in a production system. The bottleneck is now judgement rather than typing speed.
Final Thoughts
The most effective developers are not the ones generating the most code with AI. They are those making the best decisions with AI.
AI can be an extraordinary tool. It can reduce friction, accelerate lerning, uncover blind spots and eliminate repetitive work.
But it cannot replace engineering judgement. The developers who thrive in the AI era will not be those who outsource their thinking
They will be the ones who combine strong fundamentals with intelligent use of AI. In other words, they will remain developers first and AI users second.
And that’s exactly why they’ll continue to build better software than everyone else.
